notes:
apache/airflow Tests workflow, CI image checks / Static checks job (~37min). Pre-commit's `ts-compile-lint-ui` hook failed because `pnpm tsc --p <tmp tsconfig>` reported 3 TypeScript errors in 3 files: TS6196 'ColorMode' declared but never used (src/layouts/Nav/UserSettingsButton.tsx:55), TS6133 'ParamsSpec' value never read (src/pages/Connections/ConnectionForm.tsx:33), and TS2739 Type 'SetupServer' missing #private + network properties from SetupServerApi (testsSetup.ts:78). The first two are unused-import warnings raised as errors under strict TS; the third is a real type-incompatibility error. Wrapped in a Python prek helper that raises subprocess.CalledProcessError after non-zero tsc exit. Pre-commit then continues running ~30 OTHER hooks (all Pass) before the GHA step finally exits ##[error]Process completed with exit code 1.

Key property: signal_position = middle. The failure block lives at L2069-3492 of 6496 (≈32-54%), the ##[error] step exit at L3762 (≈58%), and ~42% of the log AFTER the ##[error] is post-failure pre-commit hook chatter. This is the FIRST non-late v2/stress case — selected specifically to test the 12-case 'tail unseats grep' macro finding against an axis where tail's bounded-200-line window is NOT structurally advantaged. Source URL: https://github.com/apache/airflow/actions/runs/25493092125/job/74810291973. Privacy audit: 0 hits AFTER splitting 8 overlong lines (>8000 chars each) — content was just repeated long file lists from pre-commit's Original/non-YAML/TypeScript file enumeration. Original 6424 lines; post-split 6496.
root_cause_summary: apache/airflow's pre-commit `ts-compile-lint-ui` hook failed because `pnpm tsc --p <tmp tsconfig>` against the airflow UI sources reported 3 TypeScript errors that the project's strict TS config promotes to compile failures. The errors are: (1) `src/layouts/Nav/UserSettingsButton.tsx:55:6` TS6196 — `ColorMode` is declared (`type ColorMode = (typeof COLOR_MODES)[keyof typeof COLOR_MODES];`) but never used in the file; (2) `src/pages/Connections/ConnectionForm.tsx:33:1` TS6133 — `ParamsSpec` is imported (`import type { ParamsSpec } from "src/queries/useDagParams";`) but its value is never read; (3) `testsSetup.ts:78:3` TS2739 — `Type 'SetupServer' is missing the following properties from type 'SetupServerApi': #private, network` at the call site `server = setupServer(...(handlers as Array<HttpHandler>));`. The first two are dead-code warnings raised as errors under strict TS; the third is a genuine type-incompatibility error indicating an msw API or version drift between SetupServer and SetupServerApi. The Python prek helper (`scripts/ci/prek/ts_compile_lint_ui.py:62`) wraps the tsc invocation via `run_command`, sees the non-zero exit, and raises `subprocess.CalledProcessError: Command '["pnpm", "tsc", "--p", "...tmpkhdkxx97.json"]' returned non-zero exit status 2`. Pre-commit then continues running ~30 OTHER hooks (Compile / format / lint simple auth manager UI, Generate the FastAPI API spec, etc., all of which Pass), so ~42% of the log AFTER the failure block is non-failure pre-commit chatter. The GHA step finally exits with `##[error]Process completed with exit code 1` at L3762 (≈58%). The diagnoser must navigate past the initial `Compile / format / lint UI...Failed` summary at L2069 (which alone identifies the failed hook but not the root cause) into the L3391-3479 tsc error block to identify the 3 specific TS errors and the type-incompatibility shape.

job_name: Run unit tests with -race for Go packages
notes: argo-cd Integration tests workflow, 'Run unit tests with -race for Go packages' job (~43min). THREE distinct test failures: (1) cmd/argocd/commands `TestWaitOnApplicationStatus_JSON_YAML_WideOutput` (app_test.go:1936) — formatted-output assertion fails because expected timestamps are 2026-05-08T03:25:36 but actual produced 2026-05-08T03:25:35 (1-second timing flake during table rendering). (2) server `TestOIDCRefresh` (testing.go:1712) — Go data race detected during execution. (3) util/db `TestClusterRaceConditionClusterSecrets` (testing.go:1712) — second Go data race in cluster-secrets concurrent-access test. Failures span L54988→88904 (61% → 99% of the 89188-line log) — failure block is scattered across that 38% range, with individual ##[group]FAIL annotations earlier (L54988, L66058, L75102) and a consolidated `=== Failed` summary at L87473-88904. multi_failure=true. requires_repo_context=true (race-condition diagnoses need access to the underlying Go source files; the log shows WHERE races fire but not the racing code paths). **First v2 case to fill the huge log_size_bucket gap (>50k lines, was 0/29 corpus-wide through 13-case state)** and first v2 case with `signal_position=scattered` outside v2/holdout (prettier was the only prior scattered case). Selected as Batch 5 case 4. Source: https://github.com/argoproj/argo-cd/actions/runs/25534571975/job/74948102287. Privacy audit: 0 hits, complete_scan=True (after raising MAX_LINES_PER_FILE 50k → 200k in tools/audit_context_privacy.py to support huge logs). Note also: this case is in v2/stress (the prior 4 are also stress; this brings v2/stress to 6/3 — over-target but stress-bucket is supposed to be 'deliberately difficult' and a huge race-condition multi-failure case fits).
root_cause_summary: argo-cd's `Run unit tests with -race for Go packages` job had three distinct test failures across three packages, surfacing two related root-cause categories. (1) cmd/argocd/commands.TestWaitOnApplicationStatus_JSON_YAML_WideOutput at app_test.go:1936 — a formatted-output assertion failed because the test's expected output captured timestamps at one second BEFORE the actual rendering happened, so `2026-05-08T03:25:36` (expected) vs `2026-05-08T03:25:35` (actual) caused a string-equality mismatch on the wide-output formatter. This is a timing-flake / clock-snapshot bug in the test fixture, not a logic bug in the wait-on-status command itself. (2) server.TestOIDCRefresh — `testing.go:1712: race detected during execution of test` (Go race detector under `go test -race`); the test triggers an OIDC token-refresh path that accesses shared state without locking. (3) util/db.TestClusterRaceConditionClusterSecrets — second `testing.go:1712: race detected`; concurrent-access on cluster secrets storage. The package-level FAIL coverage lines (L61296 cmd/argocd/commands 48.495s, L67598 server 30.793s, L80596 util/db 38.827s) confirm three packages failed. Final `=== Failed` summary at L87473-88904 enumerates all three with file paths and the diff/race traces; ##[error]Process completed with exit code 2 at L88904.

notes: Matrix-shaped failure: cpython's `update_windows_tcltk` branch broke Unicode surrogate-pair handling in the Windows-bundled tcltk. ALL 7 Windows variants (x64/Win32/arm64 × default/free-threading × switch-case/tail-call) fail; ALL Linux/macOS variants pass (35/8/2 success/fail/skip). This is the canonical x64-switch-case Windows leg. test_tcl has 2 distinct failures (test_eval_surrogates_in_result + test_evalfile_surrogates_in_result), both `AssertionError: '<\ud83d\udcbb>' != '<ðŸ’»>'` — the new tcltk returns UTF-8 mojibake instead of surrogate pairs. Source URL: https://github.com/python/cpython/actions/runs/25473514383/job/74742066692. Categorization caveat: case.json uses test_assertion because case.schema.json doesn't yet support matrix_or_monorepo_failure (only the v2 case_tags.schema.json extension does). tags.json carries the v2 category with a 'category mismatch justified' note.
root_cause_summary: cpython's `update_windows_tcltk` branch updated the Windows-bundled tcltk to 9.0.3 (see L229: `Fetching tcltk-9.0.3.0-unsigned...`). The new tcltk returns UTF-8-encoded mojibake instead of correctly-encoded surrogate pairs when Tcl evaluates a string containing a non-BMP code point. Two test_tcl tests fail with the same shape: `tcl.eval(r'set a "<\ud83d\udcbb>"')` returns `'<ðŸ’»>'` (UTF-8 bytes interpreted as Latin-1) instead of the expected surrogate pair `'<\U0001f4bb>'` — `test_eval_surrogates_in_result` (test_tcl.py:57) and `test_evalfile_surrogates_in_result` (test_tcl.py:292). Linux/macOS use system tcl/tk and are unaffected — all non-Windows matrix legs pass. This Windows job exits 2 after a re-run also failed.

notes: nodejs/node test-macOS run, parallel test suite (5175 tests). One test failed: `parallel/test-debugger-exec` (test/parallel/test-debugger-exec.js). The test launches `out/Release/node` in debugger mode then awaits a `break in` initial-break message via `waitForInitialBreak()` in test/common/debugger.js:116. The wait timed out after 15 seconds: `Error: Timeout (15000) while waiting for /break (?:on start )?in/i` thrown from test/common/debugger.js:92. Captured debugger output shows the connection succeeded (`Debugger attached.` + `debug>` prompt visible) but the expected break-in pattern never appeared. Likely cause: macOS-arm64 runner timing flake — the debugger started but the synchronous break-on-script-start emission was delayed past the 15s limit. Test runner reports `=== 1 tests failed` and exits 2 (`##[error]Process completed with exit code 2.`). Privacy audit: 0 hits (complete_scan=True after splitting 3 overlong gyp libtool linker-command lines into shorter chunks for scanner cap; no secrets, just verbose build noise). Source URL: https://github.com/nodejs/node/actions/runs/25490868700/job/74798272222. Framework=generic because nodejs uses its own python tools/test.py runner (not pytest/jest/etc); enum lacks a `nodejs-tools-test` value. First v2 case in `timeout_or_oom` failure_category (was 0/v2). First v2 case using a per-test timeout marker (15000ms), distinct from the GHA step-level timeout we'd expect for OOM/runner-timeout.
root_cause_summary: nodejs/node parallel test `test-debugger-exec` (test/parallel/test-debugger-exec.js) failed because its `waitForInitialBreak` helper timed out waiting for the inspector debugger to emit a `break in` initial-break message. The helper, defined at `test/common/debugger.js:67-92`, awaits a regex `/break (?:on start )?in/i` against the debugger's stdout via `waitFor`. After 15 seconds without a match it throws `Error: Timeout (15000) while waiting for /break (?:on start )?in/i` from `test/common/debugger.js:92`. The captured `output` shows the debugger CLI did spin up successfully — the inspector printed `Debugger listening on ws://127.0.0.1:49508/...` and `Debugger attached.` and reached the `debug>` prompt — but the synchronous initial break that the test expects to see (the debugger should auto-pause on script start and print `break in <file>:1` in the CLI client) never appeared in the test's window. The single-test failure causes the runner to print `=== 1 tests failed` and `make: *** [test-ci] Error 1`, which propagates to GHA as `##[error]Process completed with exit code 2`. This is a macOS-arm64 runner timing flake against the debugger's break-emission timing, not a logic bug — the only signal the log gives is the timeout itself plus the partial debugger output where the connect/attach succeeded but break-in did not.

notes: Process-crash failure on numpy's `reverse-sorts` perf branch. test_datetime_nat_argsort_stability (test_datetime.py:226) calls argsort() on a NaT-containing datetime array; numpy's C extension segfaults; pytest faulthandler dumps a Python traceback and exits 245. Surface is `Fatal Python error: Segmentation fault` followed by a 30-line stack into _wrapfunc / argsort / the test. Different evidence shape from any v1.3 or prior v2 case (no assertion diff, no compile error block, no test framework FAIL marker — just a crash inside a test). Source URL: https://github.com/numpy/numpy/actions/runs/25480154290/job/74762267261. Imported on 2026-05-07.
root_cause_summary: The numpy `Linux tests / smoke_test (3.12)` job crashed with a segmentation fault inside `test_datetime_nat_argsort_stability` (numpy/_core/tests/test_datetime.py:226). The test calls `argsort()` on a NaT-containing datetime array; numpy's C extension (reached via `_wrapfunc` → `argsort` in `numpy/_core/fromnumeric.py`) segfaulted. pytest's faulthandler caught the crash, dumped a Python traceback into the test frame, and the worker exited with status 245. The job is on the `reverse-sorts` perf branch — strongly consistent with a regression in the new sort code path being exercised by NaT values. The log alone identifies WHERE the crash occurred (test name, file:line, argsort call) but cannot prove WHY without the C source for the modified sort implementation; `requires_repo_context` is therefore `true`.

notes: rust-lang/rust bors-try job (commit bce7eda69, PR #156253 'Rollup of 3 pull requests'). Compiletest assembly-llvm test `tests/assembly-llvm/wasm_exceptions.rs` failed under target `wasm32-wasip1`: FileCheck CHECK directives at L38 (`// CHECK: catch_all`) and L62 (`// CHECK: catch`) could not match in the actual emitted .s. The new wasm EH lowering emits `try_table (catch_all_ref 0)` block + `unreachable` cleanup paths (exnref-based EH spec) instead of the old `try`/`catch_all` block structure the test expected. 1 failed / 550 passed / 103 ignored in the assembly suite; bootstrap exits 1 after stage-2 test phase. Source URL: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/actions/runs/25456140797/job/74686149799. Privacy: 49 audit hits in original log resolved (2 ARTIFACTS_AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID + CACHES_AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID values redacted in place using sha256-prefix tokens; 2 GHA-already-masked AWS_*_KEY env lines stripped); re-audit clean. Categorization caveat: case.schema.json/validate_cases.py do not yet support a 'compile_check_diff' / 'assembly_check' failure_category so canonical category stays `test_assertion` (the FileCheck failure surfaces as a test-runner FAILED line). tags.json adds a free-text note flagging the assembly-diff evidence format gap. First v2 case in the rust compiler-toolchain ecosystem (non-pytest stress framework — breaks the v2/stress 2/2 pytest monoculture flagged by tools/check_split_balance.py). Largest v2 raw.log so far at 31110 lines / 2.96 MB.
root_cause_summary: Compiletest assembly-llvm test `tests/assembly-llvm/wasm_exceptions.rs` failed under target `wasm32-wasip1` because two FileCheck directives no longer match the rustc-emitted .s. The test source asserts `// CHECK: catch_all` (test_tcl source line 38) and `// CHECK: catch` (line 62) immediately after a `call may_panic` instruction. The actual emitted assembly (visible in the `Input was: <<<<<<` dump in the FAIL block) uses the newer wasm exception-handling lowering: a `try_table (catch_all_ref 0)` block with an `exnref` local for `test_cleanup`, and `try_table (catch __cpp_exception 0)` for `test_rtry`. After the call instruction the compiler emits cleanup paths ending in `unreachable` rather than the old `catch`/`catch_all` block instructions the test expected. FileCheck reports `error: CHECK: expected string not found in input` at both check sites and points to `unreachable` as the `possible intended match`. This produces 1 failed / 550 passed / 103 ignored in the assembly compiletest suite, which causes Bootstrap to abort with `Bootstrap failed while executing --stage 2 test ... tests/assembly-llvm` and the GHA step to exit 1 (##[error]Process completed with exit code 1.). The root cause is a code-change-vs-test-expectation drift in the bors-try rollup PR #156253: rustc/LLVM now emits exnref-based wasm EH (`try_table` + `catch_all_ref`) but the wasm_exceptions.rs CHECK directives still reference the legacy `try`/`catch_all` block-instruction shape.
